title: State Bank Of India vs Mormal on 10 September, 2013
body: State Bank Of India vs Mormal on 10 September, 2013 IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE FOR RAJASTHAN AT JAIPUR BENCH, JAIPUR JUDGMENT S.B. CIVIL MISC. APPEAL NO. 1045/2004 STATE BANK OF INDIA VS. MORMAL. DATE OF JUDGMENT : 10.09.2013 HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE NARENDRA KUMAR JAIN-II Mr. Virendra Singh Yadav, for the appellant. None present for the respondent. In this appeal, the judgment and decree dated 15.01.2004 passed by the Additional District Judge, No. 1, Alwar(hereinafter referred to as the First Appellate Court) in Civil Appeal No. 37/2001, Mormal Vs. State Bank of India is under challenge, whereby the appeal of the appellant-plaintiff therein was allowed and the judgment and decree dated 31.05.2001 passed by Additional Civil Judge(J.D.) & Judicial Magistrate No. 3, Alwar(hereinafter referred to as the Trial Court) in Civil Suit No. 193/1997, dismissing the suit of the plaintiff Mormal with no order as to costs, was set aside and the suit was remanded back to the Trial Court with the direction to reconsider the case as per the law and the defendant-bank was directed to file statement of account afresh and thereafter the Trial Court was further directed to pass a judgment afresh after hearing the arguments of both the sides and considering the legal position, but without being affected by the Appellate Court's order. 2. Brief facts of the case are that the plaintiff-respondent, Mormal filed a suit against the defendant-appellant, State Bank of India for rendition of account with the averments that the plaintiff by profession was an uneducated agriculturist and he had taken a loan of Rs. 67,000/- for purchasing a tractor on 01.06.1982 and the account was opened in his name with the appellant-Bank. He further pleaded that with regard to said loan, the plaintiff honestly paid the loan amount from time to time and the plaintiff had deposited the entire amount and in that process, he had deposited excess amount with the appellant-bank. The plaintiff had gone to the defendant-bank for explaining the account and also for giving the copy of statement of account with regard to the excess amount deposited by him, but the defendant did not explain the same, nor gave the copy of the statement of account of the plaintiff. Thereafter, the plaintiff served a notice dated 29.09.1993, which was not replied by the defendant-bank, nor sent the copy of statement of account and on the other hand, the plaintiff was informed vide letter dated 04.10.1993 that in his account Rs. 8941.50 were outstanding, which he was liable to pay to the bank. The plaintiff gave reply to the said letter on 04.11.1993 and again requested to give copy of the statement of account, but the defendant did not give the same and refused on 21.12.1993, due to which it became necessary for the plaintiff to file the suit. The plaintiff also averred that he had come to know that the amount deposited by him was not fully accounted in his account and interest and other expenses were arbitrarily added in his loan account. So, the plaintiff was entitled to know the details of his loan account from the defendant-bank and if there was any outstanding, then he was ready to deposit the same, but in case if the excess amount had been deposited by the plaintiff in his loan amount, then the same is liable to be paid back to him along with the interest. So, the plaintiff prayed that the suit filed by him be decreed along with the cost. 3. The defendant filed written statement in the suit stating therein that the suit was filed on wrong facts and the plaintiff, at the time of taking of the loan, had entered into an agreement for hypothecation in favour of the defendant-bank and it was agreed that 12.5% interest was to be charged on six monthly basis and the plaintiff had not repaid the principal amount as well as interest as per the agreement and at the time of filing of the suit up to 31st December, 1995, Rs. 13,882.75 were outstanding in the category of principal as well as interest and also from that date interest payable was also to be added. The defendant-bank further stated that there was no excess amount deposited by the plaintiff and when the plaintiff had come to the defendant-bank, he was explained his account. The plaintiff deposited Rs. 8,908/- with the defendant-bank on 15.07.1991 and he was explained his account and thereafter, the plaintiff did not come back to the bank to deposit rest of the amount, nor had asked for the copy of statement of account. The defendant-bank also stated that the plaintiff was many times asked to deposit the rest of the amount and the plaintiff gave a notice to the defendant-bank on imaginations and on 29.09.1993, reply to the notice was given by the defendant and the plaintiff was informed that on 27.09.1993, Rs. 8941.50 were outstanding against him, which he was required to deposit in the defendant-bank, but he did not do so. The plaintiff did not come to the bank on 20.12.1993, nor asked for the statement of account and the plaintiff had filed the suit for unnecessarily harassing the defendant, nor any cause of action has arisen in favour of the plaintiff. So, the defendant-bank prayed that the suit was liable to be dismissed with cost. 4. On the basis of the pleadings of the parties, four issues were framed in the suit on 18.10.1997, which reads thus: (i) Whether the plaintiff was entitled to ask for rendition of account for his loan account No. 118/1. (ii) Whether the plaintiff had deposited excess amount in his loan account. (iii) Whether the defendant was entitled to receive special damages of Rs. 2,000/-. (iv) Relief. 5. The Trial Court had given opportunity to both the parties to lead the evidence. The plaintiff got examined himself as P.W.1 and in support of his case exhibited some documents and from the side of defendant-bank, Mr. Lalit Kumar was examined as D.W.1 and the documents were also got exhibited. After hearing both the parties, learned Trial Court decided Issue No. 1 against the plaintiff, wherein it was admitted position that the plaintiff had taken loan of Rs. 67,000/- and some documents were also executed. The plaintiff had admitted to execute the documents in favour of the defendant-bank for taking the loan. As per the loan agreement, interest chargeable was 12.5% on half yearly basis and in default compound interest was chargeable and for that the plaintiff did not dispute that he had put his signatures on the documents. The contention of the plaintiff was that the interest charged at half yearly rests basis was incorrect and could not be charged as per the judgment delivered by the Hon'ble Apex Court in the case of Corporation Bank Vs. D.S. Godwa , (1994) 5 SCC 213. The learned Trial Court held in Issue No. 1 that the plaintiff was not entitled for rendition of account as well as copy of statement of account by way of opening of the account again. In Issue No. 2 also learned Trial Court held that no excess amount has been deposited by the plaintiff with the defendant-bank. Issue No. 3 was decided in favour of the plaintiff holding that the defendant was not entitled for special damages of Rs. 2,000/- and in relief, as Issue Nos. 1 and 2 were decided against the plaintiff, the suit of the plaintiff was dismissed without any order as to costs by the learned Trial Court vide judgment and decree dated 31.05.2001. 6. Feeling aggrieved by the judgment and decree dated 31.05.2001 passed by the learned Trial Court, the plaintiff preferred an appeal before the First Appellate Court, which was allowed by the learned First Appellate Court vide its judgment and decree dated 15.01.2004 setting aside the judgment and decree dated 31.05.2001 passed by the learned Trial court and the case was remanded back to the learned Trial Court for reconsideration and the defendant-bank was also directed to present the statement of account after preparing it afresh and the learned Trial Court was further directed to hear the arguments of both the sides afresh as per law and to decide the case without being affected by the judgment of the First Appellate Court. Being aggrieved by the judgment and decree dated 15.01.2004 passed by the First Appellate Court, the defendant-appellant-bank has preferred instant appeal. 7. Initially, second appeal was filed before this Court, but thereafter, an application under Section 151 CPC came to be filed for converting the S.B. Civil Second Appeal into S.B. Civil Misc. Appeal under Order 43 Rules 1 and 2 CPC . Thereafter, vide order dated 09.06.2004 passed by this Court, the said second appeal was treated as civil misc. appeal. 8. Heard learned counsel for the appellant-defendant, State Bank of India. Despite service, no one is present on behalf of the respondent-plaintiff. 9. Learned counsel for the appellant-bank submitted that impugned judgment and decree dated 15.01.2004 passed by the First Appellate Court is against the facts and record of the case and therefore, the same is illegal, perverse and contrary to the established principles of law and as such, the same is liable to be quashed and set aside. Learned First Appellate Court has committed a serious error of law in allowing the appeal filed by the plaintiff-respondent in cursory manner and also violated the provisions of Order 41 Rule 31 CPC . Learned First Appellate Court has allowed the appeal of the plaintiff without proper discussions and wrongly relying upon the decision delivered by the Hon'ble Apex Court in the case of Corporation Bank (supra), which is not applicable in the present case, as the facts of the case of Corporation Bank (supra) were totally different from the facts of the present case. Learned Appellate Court has held that the interest on the agricultural loan could not be charged on half yearly rests, as held in the case of Corporation Bank (supra) and the same should have been charged on annual basis. The Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of Corporation Bank (supra) has held that in the State of Karnataka from where the case had arisen, there were long duration crops of sugarcane and, therefore, the farmers were fluid on long term basis, as such looking to the fluidity of the farmers, the interest should have been charged on annual basis rather than half yearly basis, but in the instant case, the facts are totally different and in the area where the plaintiff lives, three crops are taken in a year and there are no long duration crops. Hence, it is improper application of ratio of the case by the learned First Appellate Court and, therefore, the judgment and decree passed by the First Appellate Court is liable to be quashed and set aside. 10. Learned counsel for the appellant has further argued that learned First Appellate Court has not appreciated an important aspect of the case that at the time of taking of loan of Rs. 67,000/-, the plaintiff had executed loan documents and according to those documents including hypothecation agreement, interest was payable @ 12.5% per annum on half yearly rests and the defendant in no case could have charged the interest on annual basis, going away from the terms and conditions of the loan agreement. The plaintiff was trying to take a somersault in the Court, firstly agreeing to pay loan amount back with interest @ 12.5% per annum chargeable on half yearly rests and thereafter, pleading before the Court that the interest was not chargeable on half yearly rests and was chargeable on annual basis. Thus, the plaintiff was unnecessarily trying to drag the defendant into litigation and the suit was rightly dismissed by the learned Trial Court on the ground that the plaintiff had sufficient opportunities to look into his account and also could understand the account with the help of the defendant and he has further unnecessarily reopened his account. So, Issue No. 1 was rightly decided against the plaintiff by the learned Trial Court, but the learned First Appellate Court seems to have taken unnecessary sympathetic view in favour of the plaintiff-respondent and has held that the plaintiff was entitled to relief because as per the statement of account filed by the bank showing interest chargeable on half yearly rests and as such the plaintiff was entitled for rendition of account as well as copy of statement of account as on agricultural land, interest could not have been charged on half yearly rests along with compound interest, which was against the various instructions issued by the Reserve Bank of India as well as the judgment delivered by the Hon'ble Apex Court. The Reserve Bank of India has also issued instructions wherein it has directed that the branches can charge interest on loan amount at quarterly or half yearly rests looking to the guiding factor being liquidity of fund with the borrower flowing out of production assets/marketing of his crops. Here is the case where the plaintiff belongs to the most fertile district of the State where as many as three crops are taken and the farmer is fluid three times in an year and at least two times definitely. So, the interest could not have been charged on annual rests, as in the case of Corporation Bank (supra) wherein the Hon'ble Supreme Court had delivered the judgment looking to the circumstances of the Karnataka State, where sugarcane crop is a long duration crop and the farmer is fluid only once in an year. So, the judgment and decree passed by the learned First Appellate Court is liable to be quashed and set aside. 11. Learned counsel for the appellant-bank contended that it was not a matter of Rs. 8914.50, but the same was the matter of policy for the bank, as if the defendant had charged the interest at annual rates in the case of the plaintiff, then the same would effect all other borrowers, therefore, the judgment and decree passed by the learned First Appellate Court is of immense importance to the defendant, as it would lead to serious repercussions wanting the defendant to move away from the policy of charing interest on the agricultural loans with annual rates. Although as per the loan agreement, admittedly the interest was to be charged @ 12.5% per annum with half yearly rests. The plaintiff-respondent has not raised any objection earlier and subsequently gave a notice to the defendant-bank for rendition of the account as well as statement of account and as such objections of the plaintiff were time barred and afterthoughts and he was trying to reopen his case. The Hon'ble Supreme Court in the case of State Bank of India Vs. Yasangi Venkateswara Rao , AIR 1999 SC 896 has also held that in no case the court could possibly interfere to reduce the rate of interest agreed to. So, learned counsel for the appellant-bank prayed to allow the present appeal and set aside the judgment and decree passed by the First Appellate Court and to restore the judgment and decree passed by the learned Trial Court. 12. I have given my thoughtful consideration to the submissions made by learned counsel for the appellant-defendant-State Bank of India and carefully gone through the record and judgment of both the Courts below as well as case laws and circulars of Reserve Bank of India cited and produced by learned counsel for the appellant-bank. 13. The only question requires for consideration by this Court in this case is whether in the facts and circumstances of the present case the learned First Appellate Court was justified in remanding the case of the plaintiff to the learned Trial Court with specific order that the learned Trial Court shall not be affected from the judgment of the learned First Appellate Court, while deciding the case of the plaintiff afresh? 14. Vide impugned judgment and decree passed by the learned First Appellate Court, the case was remanded to the learned Trial Court with the direction to calculate the interest on the amount of Rs. 67,000/-, which has been advanced to the respondent-plaintiff @ 12.5% per annum with half yearly interest and the direction has been issued to calculate the rate of interest on the basis of the judgment delivered in the case of Corporation Bank (supra). Learned counsel for the appellant has vehemently further contended that law laid in the case of Corporation Bank (supra) has been changed in another judgment delivered by the Hon'ble Apex Court in the case of State Bank of India(supra), in which the Hon'ble Apex Court has held as under: 7. We are unable to understand as to how the High Court could come to the conclusion that the Parliament had no jurisdiction to enact Section 21A . There can be no doubt that Section 21A deals with the question of the rate of interest, which can be charged by a banking company. Entry 45 of List I of the Seventh Schedule clearly empowers the Parliament to legislate with regard to banking. The enactment of Section 21A was clearly within the domain of the Parliament. The said Section applies to all types of loans which are granted by a banking company, whether to an agriculturist or a non-agriculturist, and, therefore, reference by the High Court to Entry 30 of List II was of no consequence. In our opinion, the said Section 21A had been validly enacted. 8. We also find it difficult to agree with the observation of the High Court that normally when a security is offered in the case of mortgage of property, charging of compound interest would be regarded as excessive. Entering into a mortgage is a matter of contract between the parties. If the parties agree that in respect of the amount advanced against a mortgage compound interest will be paid, we fail to understand as to how the court can possibly interfere and reduce the amount of interest agreed to be paid on the loan so taken. The mortgaging of a property is with a view to secure the loan and has no relation whatsoever with the quantum of interest to be charged. 15. In instant civil misc. appeal challenge before this Court is in fact a remand order passed by learned First Appellate Court. So, it is clear that it does not decide the rights of the parties at this stage; and secondly, opportunity of hearing has already been given to the parties to place their case before the Trial Court. 16. In fact, in a case like present one where the rights of the parties are yet to be decided and opportunity has been granted to the parties to place their matter including the judgment relied upon before the Court, then in such event, I failed to understand what is there to interfere in such order. It is a settled law that remand order never decides rights of the parties. Looking to the order passed by the First Appellate Court it is needless to observe that the Trial Court shall pass judgment and decree strictly in accordance with law after hearing the arguments of both the sides and considering the legal position and without being affected by the First Appellate Court's judgment, to pass the judgment afresh. 17. If that be so, then depending upon the outcome of the order/judgment that may be passed by the learned Trial Court, the parties would be at liberty to raise all objections by availing the remedy, as prescribed under the law against the aforesaid adverse order/judgment. So far as the appellant herein is concerned, it is also granted the opportunity to raise all objections both on facts and law before the concerned Court, in case the appellant suffers any adverse judgment/order from the learned Trial Court in the matter. It is obvious that the appellant-bank will be entitled to rely on any judgment which is in it's favour for calculation of interest. Merely, because the First Appellate court has directed the Trial Court to calculate the interest in the light of the judgment and order passed by the Hon'ble Apex Court in the Case of Corporation Bank (supra), other case laws on which the appellant-bank wants to rely obviously cannot be precluded from consideration by the Trial Court. So, anxiety of the the appellant-bank is totally unsustainable. 18. Therefore, I see no reason to allow the appellant-bank to feel aggrieved of the judgment and decree passed by the learned First Appellate Court remanding the case to the learned Trial Court for the aforesaid reasons. On the contrary, the appellant-bank itself will be benefited, if the interest is calculated, as in that event the bank can also realise the amount, which is finally held payable by the plaintiff-respondent. 19. Subject to aforesaid discussions, instant civil misc. appeal filed by the appellant-bank fails and is, hereby, dismissed with no order as to costs. 20. A copy of this order be sent to the learned Courts concerned immediately for information and further necessary action. (NARENDRA KUMAR JAIN-II),J. Manoj. All corrections made in the judgment/order have been incorporated in the judgment/order being emailed. MANOJ NARWANI JUNIOR PERSONAL ASSISTANT
